 
This product has gone through rigorous quality control tests at the factory before 
shipment. If the package is damaged during shipping, please contact your dealer. 
 
The accessories produced by Delta are only for using with Delta drive. Do not use with 
other drive to prevent damage. Do not use accessories which are not produced or 
recommended by Delta on Delta motor.

Chapter 1 Introduction PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
3 
 
1-1 Product Description 
Introducing PMa-SynRM MSI series, which are designed for fan/ pump vector control drives. The Delta 
PMa-SynRM have specialized Motor-drive integrate improvement functions to provide efficient output 
when working with Delta MS300 and CP2000 motor drives.

Chapter 1 Introduction PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
5 
 
1-5 Common Motor Standard 
Item 
Specifications 
Power Supply 
The motor is equipped with a 380V three-phase motor drive. The input 
voltage of the motor drive cannot be lower than 380V, and the maximum 
cannot be higher than the allowable maximum input value of the motor drive. 
The rated output current of the motor drive cannot be lower than the rated 
current of the motor. The carrier frequency of the motor drive cannot be lower 
than 4kHz. 
Rotation Direction 
Clockwise direction when observing from the shaft end. 
Working Environment 
Temperature: -15°C–40°C; altitude below 1000m; humidity <95%TGem 
Electrical Operating 
Conditions 
During the operation of the motor, the deviation of the power supply voltage  
and the rated frequency should be in accordance with the regulations of 
IEC60034-1 (GB755). 
Motor Work Mode 
Motor is of continuous duty (S1). 
Protection Level 
IP55 
Motor Thermal Protection KTY84-130/PTC-130 
Shaft Type 
F80-F160: Deep groove ball bearing with seals or shields 
F180-F225: Deep groove ball bearing 
Insulation Class 
Class F 
Temperature Rise Class Class B for general series 
Painting 
Anti-rust treatment, Phenolic Resin, Black topcoat (Munsell N/1.5), thickness 
of painting: above 60um. 
Operation Environment Indoor only 
Driving Method 
(1) Standard model and direct coupling only (FPC connector) 
(2) Pulley applications: Enlarged front bearing is required (See code 9= A) 
and follow table 3-2 to apply forces. 
Encoder 
N/A 
Overload Capacity 
Take 120% overload capacity for 60 seconds at rated rotation speed.  
International Certificates CE, UKCA

2-2 Storage 
2-2-1 Storage Precautions 
When the motor is not in immediate use, take the following storage precautions to ensure that the 
motor is not exposed to moisture, dust and dirt, or malfunction or damaged due to careless treatment. 
Climate, storage time and the storage facilities will all affect whether the taken precautions are 
sufficient. The malfunction or damage caused by improper storage is not covered under warranty, 
even if the motor is installed on site but has not been put into use. Be sure to observe the following 
storage precautions and save complete maintenance and repairing records. These records will also 
affect whether the motor is suitable for powering operation. Components related to assembly, such as 
fixed surface, parts, especially shaft ends, keys, shaft center holes, etc., should be greased to prevent 
rust, also prevent damage and deformation caused by impact from external objects and loss its 
original function. 
2-2-2 Storage Environment 
⚫ 
The ideal storage environment should be clean, warm, and well ventilated. 
⚫ 
The ideal ambient temperature should be 10–50°C. 
⚫ 
The motor should be storage in an environment without moisture and not too hot (for example, 
near a boiler) or too cold (for instance, near a freezer). 
⚫ 
It must be a dry and well-ventilated indoor environment, without direct sunlight, little dust, no 
corrosive gases (chlorine, sulfur, hydrogen, and its oxides, etc.) and smoke. Make sure there are 
no pests or insects such as termites, also no flooding is considered. 
⚫ 
You can use a pallet to raise the motor to prevent moisture and ground contamination. 
⚫ 
If the motor has to be stored outdoors for specific reasons, it must be covered by a well 
waterproof canvas to prevent damage from external objects or other contamination, but good 
ventilation must still be maintained. Such temporary storage should be kept as short as possible 
(no more than one month) to reduce functional differences in the future.  
⚫ 
For water-cooling motors or motors with water-cooling bearings, make sure that the water in the 
pipes has been completed removed without any residue before storage, to avoid rust and 
corrosion of the pipes or damage due to freezing during long-term storage. 
2-2-3 Moisture Protection 
When the motor is installed in a humid environment, the temperature difference after shutdown will 
cause condensation inside the motor. The water vapor condenses on the winding and bearing 
surfaces will ultimately lead to insulation failure and bearing corrosion. Since the moisture is 
extremely harmful to electrical components, the motor must be maintained at 3°C above the dewpoint 
by internal or external heating. The Delta Solution uses the drive DC operation to heat the coil (rotor 
does not rotate), increase the temperature by 5–10°C above the ambient temperature (W = 2DL / W: 
wattage, D: stator outer diameter, L: length) and evaporate the internal condensed water to avoid 
shortening the bearing life. The rated humidity of storage and transportation must not exceed 95%.

Chapter 2 Unpacking | PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
33 
 
2-2-4 Rust Protection 
Before the motor leaves the factory, the shaft journal of the shaft end will be processed with rust 
prevention, check whether the rust protection is well maintained upon receiving the motor. If there is 
any sign of rust or the anti-rust layer has fallen off, re-apply rust protection immediately. All the 
installing surfaces, shaft ends and keys, motor foots, installing holes or threads should be applied with 
rust protection. Delta reserves the right to void the warranty if rust or moisture enters the interior due 
to failure to properly store the product in accordance with the instruction manual. 
 
2-2-5 Insulation Resistance Measurement 
 
 
When measuring the insulation resistance or within a short period of time after the 
measurement, do NOT touch the wiring terminals to avoid electric shock. If the 
main power cord is still connected on the terminal block on the motor, make sure 
the power has been turned off and the motor has stopped rotating. 
 
Take proper protection for live parts to avoid the risk of electric shock. 
During storage, the insulation resistance of the motor should always be kept above the specified 
value. 
a. Refer to the following regulation of Insulation Resistance Measurement for the measured value of 
insulation resistor. 
b. Measure the value every three months. 
c. Measure the resistance of temperature sensors (for example, PT100Ω/0°C) every three months. 
d. Use a 500 VDC megohmmeter for motors of rated voltage 380V and below (including). 
e. For new windings, moisture is usually the cause of low insulation resistance. Use appropriate 
heating methods to try the windings and use the drive to increase the insulation resistance to an 
acceptable value through DC operation. 
f. 
If the insulation resistance cannot be improved after drying, there may be other factors, please 
check again. If the problem still exists, contact Delta for further information. 
 
2-2-6 Long-term Storage 
After the motor is installed (or used for a period of time), if it is planned to be shut down for a long time 
(more than one week), follow the precautions below. 
a. Refer to Section 3-1 for motor storage environment. 
b. Refer to Section 3-5 for insulation resistance measurement. 
c. Refer to Section 3-3-4 for bearing protection. 
d. A test run is required every three months. 
e. If there is vibration in the surrounding environment, loosen the shaft end coupling first. 
f. 
For water-cooling motors or motors with water-cooling bearings, make sure that the water in the 
pipes has been completed removed without any residue before storage, to avoid rust and 
corrosion of the pipes or damage due to freezing during long-term storage. 
g. Make written records of maintenance during storage as a basis for future warranty and reference.

Chapter 2 Unpacking | PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
34 
 
2-2-7 Bearing Lubrication 
Motors using rolling bearings and double shielded bearing (bearing model suffix: ZZ) are pre-greased 
and do not require re-grease. The shaft must be rotated several times once a month to ensure that 
the grease is evenly distributed inside the bearing. If the motor has been stopped for more than three 
months, run the motor at a low speed first to confirm whether the bearing can operate normally, and 
then slowly increase the speed to the original working conditions. 
 
2-2-8 Moving after Storage 
After a long period of time of shut down or storage, before the motor is powered on, a complete 
winding resistance measurement and bearing assembly inspection must be performed to check for 
rust, bearing damage and deformation. 
If the following conditions are found, make sure to properly handle the problems before putting the 
motor into use. 
a. The insulation resistance is lower than the regulated value. 
b. Rust or any signs of moisture gets inside the motor. 
c. The bearings are rusted, damaged, or deformed, etc. 
d. The motor is shut down or stored beyond the warranty period. 
e. The motor is shut down or stored in a dirty or harsh environment. 
f. 
Store the motor in areas without temperature control where the ambient temperature and humidity 
vary greatly. 
g. The motor is stopped or stored in an area subject to vibration, such as when there is heavy 
machinery or heavy traffic nearby. 
h. Store the motor outdoors.  
i. 
Keep written maintenance records to prove that the motor has been maintained and stored in 
accordance with the instructions. 
If the above conditions are found, make sure to take proper treatments before putting the motor into 
use and contact Delta if necessary. However, the maintenance/ repair required due to the above 
reasons is not included in the product warranty, and the customer must bear the relevant costs for the 
damaged parts that need to be replaced during the maintenance/ repair process.

2-3-2 Hoisting (Vertical Loading) and Transportation 
Considering the possibility of hoisting and transporting the motor and the rotary screw compressor 
together after integrated installation, it is necessary to limit the usage of the lifting eye bolts on both 
sides of the motor. The lifting eye bolts on both sides of the casing are only able to support the weight 
of the motor stator assembly and cannot support the combined weight of the rotary screw compressor 
and the whole machine. If you need to install the lifting eye bolts in the motor that are capable to 
support the weight of the entire motor and rotary screw compressor, reinstall these eye screws at the 
two holes of the rear end cover. The two lifting eye bolts of the rear end cover are able to support the 
weight when hoisting.

3-1 Installation Environment 
General standard environment of the Motor:  
⚫ 
The ambient temperature should -15 – +40°C, the relative temperature is below 95%, and the 
altitude is below 1000m. 
⚫ 
Indoor use only, no harmful gas, liquid, without dust and foreign object, no splashing, water-soaked 
environment. 
⚫ 
Fix the motor in an environment with sufficient rigidity and without resonance or impact. 
⚫ 
There should be enough space around the motor for unobstructed air intake, air exhaust and 
maintenance.  If the motor is installed in a poorly ventilated room, it is easy to cause the 
surrounding ambient temperature to rise, affecting the heat dissipation effect of the motor. 
⚫ 
Do not allow foreign objects such as various fibers, pieces of paper, wood chips (chips) or metal 
fragments to enter the motor drive or adhere to the cooling fan.

3-3-2 Installation Precautions 
1. Do not disassemble or repair the product by yourself, if there is any defect in the shell or 
malfunction of the product, contact Delta for further treatment. Self-disassembly will not be 
accepted for warranty. 
2. The load on the motor transport suspension is limited to motor body. Applying other loads by 
external force may cause unbalanced forced and overturning. In severe cases, the suspension 
may even break. 
3. Install the shaft end key before assembling the coupling to avoid falling off and grind off the 
excess shaft end key after installation to avoid vibration caused by imbalance. 
4. Confirm whether the force on the shaft end meets the bearing life requirements for belt drive, refer 
to the FAD value in the table of Section 3-3-4 Allowable Radial Force/ Axial Force. 
5. Before operation, check whether the fan inlet is blocked. The fan inlet must be cleared by at least 
the distance of the fan diameter to avoid poor air intake and motor overheating. 
6. Coupling installation: The motor and the load are transmitted through the coupling, the alignment 
of the two rotating shafts must be checked. As shown in the figure below, install a dial indicator on 
the coupling on the motor side and rotate the motor to align it with the shaft center with an 
accuracy of 0.01–0.02 mm. Secondly, the gap between the two surfaces of the connector is 
measured with a thickness gauge, etc. The gap should be adjusted to an average accuracy of 
0.01–0.02 mm. 
 
7. Before powering on and operation, make sure the grounding wire is installed and whether the 
screws are loose. 
8. The installation base must be rigid enough to avoid unnecessary vibration and noise (for example, 
metal platform or concrete platform), the flatness requirements are as the following:

Precautions for belt drive: 
1. For all belt/ chain/ gear applications, the force on the D end must NOT exceed the value 
recommended by Delta to avoid damage to the motor bearing. If the application exceeds this limit, 
consult with Delta. 
2. When installing the belt, follow the manufacturer’s recommended value for belt tension, center 
distance, number of strands and model to avoid belt slippage and burning or replacement ahead 
of normal lifespan due to belt over-tightening. 
3. As mentioned above, the motor mounting base needs a slide rail adjustment mechanism to adjust 
the tension by adjusting the bolts (or screws); an idler wheel design can adjust the tension by 
moving the idler wheel horizontally. 
4. The belt tension will loosen over time, it is necessary to regularly check whether the tension value 
is too low. 
5. When installing the pulley, it should be as close to the motor as possible to reduce bending stress. 
The center points of the driving end and the driven end of the pulley should be aligned. Pay extra 
attention to the parallelism of the rotation axis to avoid belt breakage due to skewness or torsion. 
6. There should be no belt jumping, friction, slipping, abnormal noise or abnormal vibration during 
operation. 
7. Use a tapered sleeve pully connection to fasten the pulley, in order to cope with different working 
conditions of the client and to prevent axial displacement of the pulley, key and shaft, also avoid 
stress concentration that may cause the motor slip or failing to transmit power.

3-4 Wiring Precautions 
Open the motor wiring box cover to check whether the U, V, and W terminals are clearly marked and pay 
attention to the following instructions when wiring. Do NOT make any wrong connections. 
 
 
When wiring, turn off the AC motor drive power first. It takes a certain time for the 
internal DC capacitor to discharge. To avoid damage, use a voltmeter for testing. 
Wiring only after the voltage is lower than the safety voltage value of 25 VDC. If the 
AC motor drive does not fully discharge, there will be residual voltage inside. Any 
wiring at this time causes short-circuit and fire. It is strongly suggested to operate 
the wiring under no-voltage conditions to ensure personnel safety. 
 
Only qualified persons are allowed to perform wiring. Make sure the power is 
turned off before wiring to prevent electric shock. 
 
Make sure the power is only applied to the U, V, and W terminals. Failure to 
comply may result in damage to the motor. The voltage and current must be in the 
range indicated on the nameplate (refer to Section 1-2 Nameplate Information).  
 
All units must be grounded directly to a common ground terminal to prevent 
damage from lightning strike or electric shock and reduce noise interference. 
 
Tighten the screws of the main circuit terminals to prevent sparks caused by 
screws loosened due to vibration. 
 
 
For your safety, choose wires that comply with local regulations when wiring. 
 
Check the following items after finishing the wiring: 
1.  Are all connections correct? 
2.  Are there any loosen wires? 
3.  Are there any short circuits between the terminals or to ground?

3-5 Inspection before Wiring 
3-5-1 Check the Insulation Resistor before Wiring 
 
3-5-2 When to Check the Insulation Resistor 
Insulation resistor measurement provides information of motor safety. Check the insulation resistor at 
the following time: 
⚫ 
Before first start-up of the motor 
⚫ 
After a long-term of storage or after the motor stops 
⚫ 
When performing motor maintenance 
 
From the above inspection, you can check the following status of the insulation resistor: 
⚫ 
Is the insulation layer at the end of the winding contaminated with conductive dirt 
⚫ 
Whether the winding insulation layer is damp 
 
Based on the above status inspection, you can decide whether to commission the motor or to take 
necessary treatments, such as cleaning or drying the windings: 
⚫ 
Whether the motor can run 
⚫ 
Cleaning or drying measures are required 
 
3-5-3 First Check after Installation or Maintenance 
Check the following items when the motor runs for about 5000 hours or at the latest after one year: 
⚫ 
Whether the motor operation complies with the electrical characteristic parameters 
⚫ 
If the motor is running above the allowable temperature of the coil 
⚫ 
Whether the motor runs smoothly and the motor noise does not get worse 
⚫ 
Is the motor appearance intact without cracks 
If any problem is discovered during the inspection, it must be resolved promptly to avoid damage to 
the motor. 
 
3-5-4 Routine Inspection 
The following inspections are required after the motor has been running for about 16000 hours or at 
the latest after two years: 
⚫ 
Whether the motor installation and fixation meet the requirements 
⚫ 
Whether the motor operation complies with the electrical characteristic parameters 
⚫ 
If the motor is running above the allowable temperature of the coil 
⚫ 
Whether the motor runs smoothly and the motor noise does not get worse 
⚫ 
Is the motor appearance intact without cracks 
⚫ 
The motor centering is within the allowable tolerance range 
⚫ 
Are all mechanical and electrical fasteners tightened 
⚫ 
If the cables and insulation components are in good condition and have not discoloration

3-6-1 Wiring Method 
⚫ 
Tri-binding Post Junction Box (Aluminum Case) 
Motor in an aluminum shell ( the tenth digit in the motor model name is A) uses a tri-binding post 
junction box. The external power supply and the U, V, W outlet wire of the motor are locked on 
the three binding posts for power transmission. The temperature sensors KTY-84 (yellow and 
green wires) and PTC-130 (double blue wires) can be used for real-time temperature monitoring 
and over-temperature protection. 
 
⚫ 
Hex-binding Posts Junction Box (Cast Iron Case) 
Motor in a cast iron shell (the tenth digit in the motor model name is B) uses a hex-binding posts 
junction box. Its front and rear conduction adopt a two-row three-column wiring design. The 
motor U1, V1, W1 outlet wires are locked to one end of the three posts, and the other end is 
locked with the external power supply U2, V2, W2 for power transmission. The temperature 
sensor KTY-84 (yellow and green wire) and PTC-130 (double blue wire) can be used for real-
time temperature monitoring and over-temperature protection.

This product has gone through rigorous quality control tests at the factory before 
shipment. If the package is damaged during shipping, please contact your dealer. 
 
The accessories produced by Delta are only for using with Delta drive. Do not use 
with other drive to prevent damage. Do not use accessories, which are not produced 
or recommended by Delta on Delta drive.

Chapter 4 Motor Operation PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
59 
 
4-1 Motor Pre-start Inspection 
You must use fasteners made of the right material and apply the right tightening torque, otherwise the 
current transfer may be deterred or connecting parts may become loose and fasteners may loosen. 
This may result in a lower than required minimum electrical gap, possibly resulting in the formation of 
sparks. 
  
The electrical connections are subject to the following precautions. 
⚫ 
Tighten the threaded connection using the specified fastening torque. 
⚫ 
Follow the material specifications of the fasteners. 
⚫ 
Check the connection when inspecting. 
⚫ 
Use the correct cable specifications. 
 
The cable specifications should be noted as follows. 
⚫ 
Rated current 
⚫ 
Rated voltage 
⚫ 
Number of service factors that may be required 
⚫ 
Equipment-related conditions, such as ambient temperature, wiring method, and cable length.

Chapter 4 Motor Operation PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
62 
 
 Basic motor parameters adjustment 
1. 
Parameter reset: 
Reset Pr.00-02 = 9 (50 Hz) or 10 (60 Hz) to the default value. 
2. 
Select PM motor type: 
Pr.05-33 = 1 (SPM) or 2 (IPM) 
3. 
Motor nameplate parameter setting: 
Parameter 
Description 
Pr.01-01 
Rated frequency (Hz) 
Pr.01-02 
Rated voltage (VAC) 
Pr.05-34 
Rated current (A) 
Pr.05-35 
Rated power (kW) 
Pr.05-36 
Rated rotor speed (rpm) 
Pr.05-37 
Number of poles for the motor (poles) 
4. 
PM parameter auto-tuning: 
Set Pr.05-00 = 5 (rolling auto-tuning for PM, with no load) or 13 (static auto-tuning for PM) 
and press RUN key to finish motor auto-tuning, then you will get the following parameters: 
Parameter 
Description 
Pr.05-39 
Stator resistance for a permanent magnet motor (Ω) 
Pr.05-40 
Permanent magnet motor Ld (mH) 
Pr.05-41 
Permanent magnet motor Lq (mH) 
Pr.05-43 
Ke parameter of a permanent magnet motor (Vphase，rms / krpm) 
(When Pr.05-00 = 5, the Ke parameter is measured based on the actual motor 
rotation.) 
(When Pr.05-00 = 13, the Ke parameter is automatically calculated based on 
the motor power, current and rotor speed.) 
If an auto-tuning error (AUE) occurs, refer to Chapter 14 “Fault Codes and Descriptions” for 
further treatment. 
AUE Error (code) 
Description 
AUE (40) 
Auto-tuning error 
AUE1 (142) 
Auto-tuning error 1 (No feedback current error) 
AUE2 (143) 
Auto-tuning error 2 (Motor phase loss error) 
5. 
Set control mode 
Control mode for the drive: Pr. 00-10 = 0: Speed mode 
Control mode for the motor: Pr. 00-11 = 2: PM SVC mode 
6. 
Re-power on after power off. 
7. 
Measure the initial magnetic pole angle of PM 
Set Pr.10-53 PM initial rotor position detection method 
0: Disabled 
1: Using I/F current command (Pr.10-31) to attract the rotor to zero degrees 
2: High frequency injection 
3: Pulse injection  
(For IPM, the setting value is suggested to be 2; for SPM, the setting value is suggested to 
be 3. You can choose the setting 1 if the result is not good of setting as 2 or 3.)

Chapter 4 Motor Operation PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
64 
 
 Adjustment for operation with light load 
8. 
Start the motor without load / with light load and operate to 1/2 of the rated rotor speed 
A1. Start operation direction: 
a. 
If the start operation direction is wrong 
SPM: increase the current proportion for Pr.10-42 (initial angle detection pulse 
value) to improve the accuracy of the angle detection. 
IPM: Increase the voltage for Pr.10-52 (injection magnitude) to improve the 
accuracy of the angle detection. 
b. 
If an ocA error occurs when pressing RUN to start the motor, decrease the current 
proportion for Pr.10-42 (initial angle detection pulse value).  
A2. Operates the motor in 1/2 of the rated rotor speed, adjust the no-load operating current 
If the no-load operating current exceeds 20% of the rated current, increase Pr.07-26 
(torque compensation gain) and observe the no-load operating current. 
A3. Accelerate to the rated frequency and observe if the motor operates stably. 
a. 
If the motor output rotor speed presents periodic low-frequency wave, increase 
Pr.10-34 (PM sensorless speed estimator low-pass filter gain), or increase Pr.10-
32 (PM FOC sensorless speed estimator bandwidth). 
b. 
If the output frequency reflects high frequency vibration, decrease Pr.10-34 or 
decrease Pr.10-32. 
A4. Accelerate the motor to the maximum rotor speed and observe if it operates stably. 
If the motor stalls when accelerating to the maximum rotor speed, then increase Pr.10-
34 (PM sensorless speed estimator low-pass filter gain), or increase Pr.00-17 (carrier 
frequency, you must set the carrier frequency larger than 10 times of the maximum 
output frequency)

Chapter 4 Motor Operation PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
66 
 
 Adjustment for operation with heavy load 
9. 
Load operating test 
B1. Low-frequency loading performance is below 1/10 of rated frequency: 
a. 
If the low-frequency loading performance is insufficient, or the rotor speed is not 
smooth, increase Pr.10-31 (current command of I/F mode). 
b. 
If the low-frequency current is large, decrease Pr.10-31 (current command of I/F 
mode). 
B2. Test the with-load accelerating performance: 
When the motor operates in 1/10 of rotor speed and above, if the speed cannot follow 
the acceleration time during accelerating, or the current stalls, increase Pr.07-38 
(PMSVC voltage feedback forward gain). 
10. Stability test at constant speed operation: the motor operates stably at constant speed 
a. 
If the motor output rotor speed presents periodic low-frequency wave, increase Pr.10-
34 (PM sensorless speed estimator low-pass filter gain), or increase Pr.10-32 (PM 
FOC sensorless speed estimator bandwidth). 
b. 
If the output frequency reflects high frequency vibration, decrease Pr.10-34 or 
decrease Pr.10-32.

proportion for Pr.10-42 (Initial angle detection pulse value). An excessive pulse 
current may cause ocA error easily.

b.

decrease Pr.10-32.

If the motor stalls when accelerating to the maximum rotor speed, then increase Pr.10-

Chapter 4 Motor Operation PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
74 
 
4-3 Motor Start and Operation 
 
 
If the motor does not start within 1–2 seconds, turn off the power immediately. The 
motor must be thoroughly inspected and troubleshooted before it can be started 
again. 
 
Startup Time and Abnormal Noise: The startup time will increase for large load 
machines. However, for general machine startup, if the time is particularly long or 
difficult to complete the startup, or if there is an abnormally loud noise emitted during 
the startup, do not operate the motor and contact the original manufacturer as soon 
as possible. 
After the motor has been installed, you need to run it with no load, and only after it is running normally 
you can connect it to a loaded machinery. Except under special circumstances, the motor is usually 
started with light load and slowly loaded at full speed for full load operation. When the deviation of the 
power supply voltage and frequency from the rated voltage and frequency values on the nameplate 
exceeds ±5% and ±1%, respectively, the motor cannot guarantee continuous output of the rated power, 
and continuous overloading is not allowed. 
 
If you need to start and restart the motor too frequently, pay more attention on the input current limit 
(refer to Section 1-6 Motor Characteristic Curves). You can restart the motor when the startup fails, but 
you must pay attention to the over-current protection level.  
 
The possible errors and corresponded troubleshooting are as follows:

Chapter 4 Motor Operation PMa-SynRM MSI Series 
75 
 
Rotation Direction 
a. 
Delta motor runs forward and reversely (dual rotation) 
b. 
If you need to change the rotation direction, you need to wait for the motor to stop completely and 
then exchange any two phases of the three-phase outlet wires. 
 
Power Supply, Voltage and Current  
a. 
Power supply and the voltage have to be the same as the rated voltage marked on the motor’s 
nameplate. 
b. 
The voltage is within ±10% of the rated voltage (rated frequency). Make sure also the three phases 
are balanced. 
c. 
Measure the current of each phase without load. The difference between the unloaded current and 
its average value shall not exceed ±5%. 
 
Frequency 
Frequency variation is within ±1% of the rated value (at rated voltage). 
 
Lubricating ring on the bearing 
Sliding bearings that need lubricating oil use oil rings to drive the lubricating oil.  
Therefore, during operation of the motor, you must always verify if the rotation of 
the oil ring is smooth and whether the oil is flowing normally. 
 
Temperature rising on the stator 
The motor winding temperature rising protection uses PTC-130/ KTY84-130 thermistor as the motor 
overheating protection device, which is installed inside the motor stator winding. The allowable 
temperature value: KTY84-130 (130°C derating, 145°C strip).

header: 5-1 Cleaning the Cooling Fan in the Motor Rear Hood
vector_text:
Chapter 5 User Suggestions & After-sales ServicePMa-SynRM MSI Series 
79 
 
5-1 Cleaning the Cooling Fan in the Motor Rear Hood 
 
 
To prevent fire, explosion or accidents harmful to safety, the surrounding air should 
be kept free at all times when using solvents. 
 
In narrow working places (such as coal pits), artificial respiration devices with 
centralized air supply and masks with hoses or self-contained breathing equipment 
must be provided for each user. 
 
Users must wear goggles, aprons and gloves. Solvents and their volatile gases must 
not be exposed to burning flames or sparks and should be stored in safe and 
qualified containers. 
 
During the operation, the environment should be kept clean, dry, and well-ventilated. Dust and cotton on 
the surface of the motor should be cleaned regularly (every 10 days) to keep the surface of the motor 
clean. Clean the oil dirt and foreign objects on the bottom of the air hood and the cooling fan to avoid 
affecting the cooling effect and reducing the product lifespan of the cooling fan. 
 
(A) When a motor is used for a long time, there must be dust, carbon powder, grease, etc. adhered to 
the motor. These foreign objects can easily absorb the moisture and cause insulation degradation.  
The cooling effect will be hindered and causes the temperature to rise, thus causing accidents. 
Therefore, it is suggested to perform regular inspections. 
(B) Cleaning methods and precautions: 
① 
Use air compressor and small air blower: 
a. 
Make sure that there’s no moisture in the compressed air. 
b. 
The pressure should not exceed 4kg/cm2 to avoid damaging the insulation of the coil. 
However, if the pressure is too low, the effect will be poor. The most appropriate pressure 
is about 4kg/cm2. 
② 
Vacuum cleaning: 
Vacuum cleaning can be done before or after other cleaning methods to remove dust or foreign 
objects. This is an effective way to remove dirt from the winding surface without causing dust 
to fly everywhere. The tools used for vacuum cleaning must be non-metallic to prevent damage 
to the winding insulation. 
③ 
Wiping: 
The dirt on the winding surface can be wiped off with soft, lint-free clothes. If the dirt contains 
oil, the wiping clothes can be dipped in some safe petroleum solvent (not so wet that it will drip 
out). Use inhibitory chemicals in hazardous areas. When using inhibited methyl chloroform as 
a solvent, you must be careful and remove it from the operation site immediately after use. 
Although this solvent will not burn under normal circumstances, it is still necessary to take 
appropriate health and safety measures due to its toxicity.

Chapter 5 User Suggestions & After-sales ServicePMa-SynRM MSI Series 
 
80 
 
5-2 Motor Exterior Cleaning 
 
 
When the motor is running, do not clean the outside of the motor or remove the rear 
air hood to prevent flowing dust or broken objects from being directly sucked into the 
motor. 
 
The operating environment should always be kept dry. The surface of the motor should be kept clean, 
and the air inlet of the wind cover should be clear and unobstructed.  
If any abnormalities or abnormal sounds are found while the motor is running, stop immediately for 
inspection. Do not try to start again until the fault is identified and eliminated.  
It is necessary to keep the motor well lubricated during operation.  
When the bearings have been filled with grease, no more grease is added within the allowed period. 
After 5,000 hours of continuous rotation, the motor should be disassembled and inspected to refill the 
grease (except for sealed bearings). The bearing temperature should not exceed 95°C, otherwise the 
grease should be replaced (grease according to the motor refueling mark). 
 
① 
Semi-enclosed motors are suitable for clean, dry, well-ventilated and non-corrosive environments. 
The air inlet window at the air inlet should not be filled with dirt, dust, lint and other debris, or it may 
block the air flow path and cause the air not able to flow freely. 
② 
If the motor is equipped with filter consumables, they must be replaced regularly (replaceable filter 
consumables), or the filter (permanent type) must be cleaned and maintained according to on-site 
usage conditions. If the filter is not cleaned for a long time, the filter will lose its filtering effect. 
Therefore, users must be reminded to replace or clean the filter regularly. 
③ 
For fully sealed air-cooling methods and fully sealed external fan cooling methods, special cleaning 
considerations are required for the motor. The outer fan must be cleaned of dust and dirt, because 
the dust accumulated on the outer fan will cause imbalance and vibration. The heat exchange tubes 
inside the motor's air duct cold box must be cleaned with an appropriate tube brush. The material of 
the tube brush is rigid artificial fiber (do NOT use metal steel brushes).

DRAStudio (Delta Robotic Automation Studio) is the software for operating the Delta DRS 
series and DRV series robots. This chapter provides descriptions of this software. 
You can download the DRAStudio software at Delta’s website by referring to Section A.1 Data 
search method in Appendix A.

1.2 Screen overview 
The software operation screen is divided into three areas: 
 Quick access toolbar: for the quick check of the robot status and the use of the function 
shortcut keys, such as the alarm / warning message (Alarm) and alarm reset function 
(Reset). 
 Main page: the main area for operations such as project management (Project), point 
management (Point), and program editing (RL). 
 Subpage: auxiliary functions for robot operation, such as jogging (JOG) and controller 
information (Output).

1.3.2  Robot model selection 
The 
 (Robot model selection) button shows SCARA, VA, or RS-AFS according to the type 
of the connecting robot. 
 SCARA: the DRS series 3-axis, 4-axis, and 5-axis robots. 
 VA: the DRV series 6-axis robots. 
 RS-AFS: screwdriving robots. 
Selecting the robot model: 
Click 
 (Robot model selection), and the screen displays the window for selecting the 
robot model. 
 
 
 Manual model selection 
Refer to the model number on the nameplate of the robot to select the model.

1.3.3  Connection 
Click 
 (Connection) to display the Connect window. There are two communication interfaces 
available for connecting the Delta robot to the software: 
 Ethernet 
Ethernet connection. Use the RJ45 cable to connect the robot with the computer. In this 
case, you can operate the robot through the software. 
 USB1 
USB connection. Use the USB Type B cable to connect the robot with the computer. In 
this case, you can only check the IP address of the robot controller through the software. 
 
In the IP fields*Note, enter the IP address of the robot controller. The default IP address is 
192.168.1.1. 
 
Note: the computer and the connecting robot must be within the same domain. Thus, you need to set the 
computer’s IP address to 192.168.1.XX when connecting to a new robot. 
 
Click Connect, and the connection between the software and the robot is established.

when the servo is On.

There are dedicated teach pendants (TP) for different series of Delta robots. The

status) icon shows whether the robot is controlled by the TP. 
In DRAStudio, you can only check the TP status. Granting or taking the control is only available 
on the TP. When the TP is in control of the robot operation, you cannot operate the robot (such 
as jogging or project execution) through DRAStudio.

T1 (Test mode 1) 
For point teaching. In this mode, you can only operate the robot at low speed. 
 Jog speed 
The TCP (Tool Center Point) linear speed is limited under 225 mm/sec. 
 Moving speed during project execution 
When the set moving speed in the project is above 225 mm/sec, the speed is limited at  
225 mm/sec. 
For example, when the moving speed is set to 500 mm/sec in the program, the actual 
moving speed is 225 mm/sec. 
 Functional pause (System DI2) 
In T1 mode, whether the System DI2 is On or Off does not trigger the functional pause. 
 
 
 

Software Introduction                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
1-12 
1 
T2 (Test mode 2) 
For project testing. In this mode, you can operate the robot at high speed. 
 Jog speed 
The TCP (Tool Center Point) linear speed is limited under 225 mm/sec. 
 Moving speed during project execution 
Based on the maximum speed of the model. 
 Functional pause (System DI2) 
When the System DI2 is Off, the functional pause is triggered. To clear the warning, set 
both the System DI2 and DI3 to On. 
 
Auto (Auto mode) 
 Jog speed 
The TCP (Tool Center Point) linear speed is limited under 225 mm/sec. 
In Auto mode, the JOG function is only operable in DRAStudio but not on the TP. 
 Moving speed during project execution 
Based on the maximum speed of the model. 
 Functional pause (System DI2) 
When the System DI2 is Off, the functional pause is triggered. To clear the warning, set 
both the System DI2 and DI3 to On.

1.5.7  Function 
In the Function tab, you can use the Modbus reading / writing function. Select the check box for 
the specified item, enter the specified address, select the Word size (W or DW), and then click 
Write to write the value to the controller or click Read to read the value from the controller. 
 
There are three subtabs within the Function tab: 
Subtab 
Address 
Description 
Modbus Read Only 
0x0000 - 0x0FFF 
Internal memory parameters. The parameter values in 
this tab are read-only and write-protected. 
Modbus R/W 
0x1000 - 0x1FFF 
Volatile parameters. You can read and write values in 
this tab. Data in the memory addresses is cleared to 0 
after power off. 
Device Latch 
0x3000 - 0x3FFF 
Non-volatile parameters. You can read and write 
values in this tab. Data in the memory addresses is 
retained after power off. 
When you select W for the Word size, one value takes one address, such as 0x0000, 0x0001, 
0x0002 and so on. When you select DW for the Word size, one values takes two addresses, 
such as 0x0000, 0x0002, 0x0004 and so on.

box as follows). Drag the slider to change the override setting between 1% and 100%. Adjusting 
the override affects the speed of robot jogging and program running.

function is not available, with the slider shown as follows.

2.3 Mode selection 
 
Select the Mode to switch between coordinate frames. There are four types of modes 
(coordinate frames), including the Joint frame mode, World frame mode, User frame mode, and 
Tool frame mode. Select the proper mode to operate the robot according to the applications. 
When selecting User or Tool for the Mode, you can specify the User Frame ID (UF) and Tool 
Frame ID (TF).

Joint for the Mode, when a standard SCARA robot is connected, the controllable axes are J1 to 
J4; when a VA robot (articulated robot) is connected, the controllable axes are J1 to J6.

3.2 
Create a project 
Create a new project, and the project is stored in the corresponding folder; you can only create 
projects on the PC. Follow these steps. 
1. Right-click the [DROEsolution] folder in the PC project list, and then click Add New RL 
Project. 
 
2. Specify a project name, and then click OK. 
Note: only English letters and numbers can be used for naming the project; symbols and spaces are 
not allowed. The maximum length of the project name is 26 characters. 
 
3. After the project is created, a folder with the specified project name is added to the PC 
project list, containing the files LPT.bin and main.lua.

Language Manual.

saved.

3.8 
User Library 
The User Library contains a collection of functions that you can define based on the 
requirements. Create a User Library file on the PC and upload the file to the controller, and then 
you can call the user-defined functions in every project. 
Important: 
 
You need to upload the User Library file to the controller before calling the user-defined 
functions. 
 
When a function in the User Library shares the same name with a function in the main 
program or a built-in instruction of the controller, the priority of execution would be:  
main program function > User Library function > controller’s built-in instruction. 
 
When multiple functions in the User Library share the same name, the system only 
executes the last loaded function. 
How to add a User Library file 
1. 
Right-click the PC project (Project5 as the example). Click Add → New RL to create a 
User Library file (.lua). 
Note: do not name the User Library file as main or include any symbols or spaces, otherwise the file 
cannot be uploaded to the controller. 
 
2. 
Write the user-defined functions in the User Library file. In this case, you can only call 
these self-defined functions in Project5. 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                Project 
3-15 
3 
3. 
If you desire to use this User Library file in all the projects, right-click the [UserLibrary] 
folder in the PC project list, and then click Add Exists. 
 
4. 
Select the file to be added. 
 
5. 
Then, the system automatically copies the selected file to the [UserLibrary] folder under 
the installation folder. Meanwhile, DRAStudio updates the UserLibrary list of the PC. 
6. 
Upload the User Library file in the [UserLibrary] folder to the controller. 
 
7. 
After the file is successfully uploaded, the user-defined functions in this User Library file 
can be called in every project. 
 

Project                                                                DRAStudio User Manual 
3-16 
3 
How to delete a User Library file 
Follow these steps: 
1. 
Right-click the User Library file in the controller, and then click Delete to delete the file. 
 
2. 
Go to the [UserLibrary] folder under the installation folder and delete the corresponding 
file. 
 
3. 
Right-click the [UserLibrary] folder in the PC project list, and click Update to update the 
UserLibrary list. You can see that the deleted file has been removed from the list. 
 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                Project 
3-17 
3 
How to encrypt a User Library file 
Right-click the User Library file in the PC project list, and click Encrypt to protect the project 
with a password. The project file becomes locked and can only be viewed after being unlocked. 
Only English letters and numbers can be used for setting the password; symbols and spaces 
are not allowed. The maximum length of the password is 16 characters. 
 
How to decrypt a User Library file 
Right-click the User Library file in the PC project list, and click Decrypt to unlock the project file 
by entering the correct password. If you forget the password, contact Delta or the distributor.

4.4 
Local points 
Each project stores its own local points. The ID of local points ranges from 1001 to 7000, which 
means up to 6,000 local points can be saved in one project. Refer to Section 4.3 Point data for 
details. 
 
Local tab in Point management page 
 
4.5 
Global points 
The global points are stored in the internal memory of the controller and shared by all projects. 
The ID of global points ranges from 1 to 1000, which means up to 1,000 global points can be 
saved in the controller. Refer to Section 4.3 Point data for details. 
 
Global tab in Point management page

The User Frame is a plane coordinate frame that can be defined on any position in space, such 
as a workpiece or a workbench. By referring to the positions of Origin, +X axis, and XY, the 
User Frame can be transformed from the World frame. The generated User Frame is as follows. 
Note: you need to set the Tool Frame n before defining the User Frame.

header: How to teach the User Frame
vector_text:
DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point 
4-25 
4 
4.6.1 
How to teach the User Frame 
1. Go to the JOG subpage. Select User for the Mode and select the TF (Tool Frame ID). 
 
 
2. Move the robot to the position to be set as the Origin of the User Frame, and then click 
Teach. 
   
 
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
4-26 
4 
3. Move the robot to any point on the +X axis to be set for the User Frame, and then click 
Teach. The further you move the robot away from the Origin, the more accurate the result. 
+X
Origin
  
 
 
4. Move the robot to any point on the XY plane to be set for the User Frame, and then click 
Teach. 
+X
XY
Origin
  
 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point 
4-27 
4 
5. After successfully teaching the Origin, +X axis, and XY, click Set to write the data to the 
controller and create the User Frame. 
Note: you can also input values directly to the table, and then click Set to create the User Frame. 
 
 
6. To check if the data is successfully written, select the ID of the User Frame you just set, and 
click Get to read the User Frame stored in the controller from the table. 
 
Important: if you select the User Frame ID which does not contain any data for performing 
jogging, then the alarm E1.82A (Switching error of user’s coordinates system) is 
triggered. 
 
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual

The Tool Frame includes a Tool Center Point (TCP) and the Tool Frame directions. The default 
TCP is defined on the flange center at the end of the robotic arm. As shown in the figures at the 
bottom of this page, the Tool Frame rotates when the end of the robotic arm rotates.

Width. The radial distance between the TCP and the flange at the end of the robotic arm.

SCARA robots, and +Z for VA robots.

4.7.1 
Teaching the tool size 
If you want to calculate the tool size by teaching, click Determine in the Tool Size area. 
 
 
The system then displays the Tool size calculation screen: 
 
 
Name 
Description 
ID 
Select the Tool Frame ID. 
Input 
Displays the points for teaching the tool size. You need to teach at least three points. 
Output 
Displays the new tool size after calculation. 
Save 
Save the points in the table. 
Teach 
Save the current position of the robot in the table. 
Clear 
Select a row in the table (which is then highlighted blue), and then click this button to 
clear the row data. 
Calculate 
Calculate the Width, Height, and Angle for the tool size. You have to first select the 
check boxes in the Select column. 
Set 
Write the calculated tool size (Width, Height, and Angle) to the selected Tool Frame ID. 
 
 
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
4-32 
4 
Method for teaching the tool size 
Select a fixed reference point. Move the tooltip to the reference point, and then teach at least 
three points with different postures to generate the tool size. Refer to the following figures. 
When teaching, make sure to move the rotating axis in all directions. Move the RZ axis for the 
4-axis SCARA robots; move the RY and RZ axes for the 5-axis SCARA robots; and move the 
RX, RY, and RZ axes for the 6-axis VA robots. 
RZ=90
RZ=180
RZ=0
 
Teaching the tool size for SCARA robots 
 
Teaching the tool size for VA robots

header: How to teach the tool size
vector_text:
DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point 
4-33 
4 
4.7.2 
How to teach the tool size 
1. Select a Tool Frame ID, and then click Determine in the Tool Size area to open the Tool 
size calculation screen. 
 
2. Select a fixed point, and then move the tooltip to the fixed point. Select the first table row, 
and then click Teach to save the current position to the row. 
  
 
3. Rotate the end effector and move the tooltip to the fixed point. Select the second table row, 
and then click Teach to save the current position to the row. 
  
 
 
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
4-34 
4 
4. Rotate the end effector again and move the tooltip to the fixed point. Select the third table 
row, and then click Teach to save the current position to the row. 
  
 
5. After teaching at least three points with different postures, select the check boxes in the 
Select column for the points to be calculated, and then click Calculate. 
 
6. After calculation, the Output area in the bottom shows the new tool size. 
 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point 
4-35 
4 
7. The Error column shows the difference between the points and the calculated TCP in units 
of mm. Make sure the difference is within the application specification. 
 
8. Click Set to write the calculated tool size (Width, Height, and Angle) to the controller. 
 
9. Click Save to save the points displayed in the Tool size calculation screen. 
 
 
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
4-36 
4 
10. Click Back to return to the Tool Frame tab. 
 
11. Select the Tool Frame ID for which you just set the too size, and then the set size is 
displayed in the Tool Size area.

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point

If you want to calculate the Tool Frame directions by teaching, click Determine in the Tool

Note: you can define the Yaw for the 4-axis SCARA robots; Pitch and Yaw for the 5-axis SCARA robots; 
Roll, Pitch, and Yaw for the VA robots. 
 
The system then displays the Tool Orientation Calculation screen: 
 
Name 
Description 
ID

Calculate the Roll (RX), Pitch (RY), and Yaw (RZ), and then write the data to the 
selected Tool Frame ID.

Displays the Tool frame directions which have been set for the selected Tool

vector_text:
DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point

4.7.4 
How to teach the Tool frame directions 
Set the coordinate frame (Mode) to World (both the User Frame ID and Tool Frame ID are set to 
0 in this mode), and then follow these steps to convert the default Tool Frame Z axis direction 
for it to align with the direction of the end effector. 
ZTF0
YTF0
XTF0
 
Default Tool Frame directions 
1. Click Determine in the Tool Orientation area to open the Tool Orientation Calculation 
screen. 
 
2. Select a fixed point, and then move the end effector to the fixed point. Select the Origin 
table row, and then click Teach to save the current position to the row. 
Origin
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
4-40 
4 
3. Move the end effector along the +X axis direction you want to set. Select the +X axis table 
row, and then click Teach to save the current position to the row. This sets the X axis 
direction of the Tool Frame. 
+X
 
4. Move the end effector along the +Y axis direction you want to set. Select the +Y axis table 
row, and then click Teach to save the current position to the row. This sets the Y axis 
direction of the Tool Frame. Then, the system is now able to calculate the Z axis direction 
after both X and Y axis directions are determined. 
+Y
 
 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point 
4-41 
4 
5. Click Tool Orientation Set to calculate and write the new Tool Frame directions to the 
controller. 
 
6. The converted Tool Frame directions are as follows. 
+Y
+X
+Z

4.8 
Work Space 
The Work Space tab in the Point management page allows you to define the workspace of the 
robot as a working area or restricted area as needed to protect the robot and the peripheral 
devices. 
 
Name 
Description 
ID 
Set the Work Space ID (ranges 1 to 9). 
Type 
Set the workspace as a Rectangle or Cylinder. Refer to the following two 
pages for details. 
Status 
Enable or Disable the selected workspace. 
Area 
Set the workspace as a Working Area or Restricted Area. 
Work Space is Closed / 
Work Space is Opened 
Displays the status of the workspace function. Click to enable or disable all 
workspace. 
Restricted Area DO 
Enable or Disable outputting the DO status of the restricted area. 
You can set to have one of the DOs among DO1 - DO12 be triggered to ON 
(light on) once the robot enters the restricted area. 
Reset 
Clear all the setting values of the current workspace. 
Get 
Read the setting values of the current workspace from the controller and 
refresh the page. 
Set 
Write the setting values of the selected Work Space ID to the controller. 
 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                       Point 
4-43 
4 
The detailed descriptions for the types of workspace are as follows. 
◼ Rectangle 
Refer to the following figure. Create a rectangular workspace by clicking the Teach button in the 
Point operation areas each time after moving the robot to the positions PO, PX, and PY. Set the 
workspace as a Working Area or Restricted Area in the Work Space tab, and then click Set 
to write the settings to the controller. 
 
 
PO
PX
PY
 
Teaching a rectangular workspace 
 
 

Point                                                                       DRAStudio User Manual 
4-44 
4 
◼ Cylinder 
Refer to the following figure. Create a cylindrical workspace by clicking the Teach button in the 
Point operation areas to input the positions of X, Y, and Z and then manually input the Radius 
(R) and Height (H). Set the workspace as a Working Area or Restricted Area in the Work 
Space tab, and then click Set to write the settings to the controller. 
Important: setting the height of the cylinder to a positive value indicates downward direction; a 
negative value indicates upward direction. 
 
 
 
 
H
R
(X, Y, Z)

Program (RL)                                                               DRAStudio User Manual 
5-2 
5 
The 
 RL page in the menu is divided into four areas: 
 
◼ Function bar: includes the functions such as Start, Pause, and Stop, and the editor shortcut 
keys such as Copy, Paste, and Search/Replace. 
◼ Editor area: the main area to write the program. 
◼ RL shortcut key: located in the RL tab of the Subpage. The RL shortcut keys allow the user 
to quickly add RL instructions when writing the program. 
◼ Output panel: displays the output variable values, strings, syntax errors, as well as the 
timestamps. 
 
 
Editor area 
Output panel 
RL shortcut key 
Function bar

DRAStudio User Manual                                                               Program (RL) 
5-3 
5 
5.1  Function bar 
The following provides descriptions of the Function bar in the RL page. 
 
Icon 
Name 
Description 
 
Open 
Open an RL script file. 
 
Save 
Save the file. 
 
Compile 
Compile the program for debugging. The robot does not 
move during compilation. 
 
Start 
Start running the program until reaching the end or the 
next breakpoint. 
After you click Start, the system first checks for errors in 
the program. Then, the program starts if no errors exist.  
If there are errors, debug the program until no errors

6.1.1 System DI 
The System DIs allow you to perform safety and program operations on the robot through 
external signals. 
 
 
E-STOP (Emergency Stop) 
You can trigger the System DI1 (E-STOP function) through the SYS.DIO connector on the 
SCARA robot controller, the Safety connector on the VA robot controller, or the E-STOP 
button on teach pendants (TP). 
 
When the E-STOP function is triggered, the robot stops immediately, and the system stops 
running the current project and displays E#013 (# indicates the axis code). 
 
Alarm / Warning 
E#013 (Safety Stop) 
Probable Cause 
The E-STOP function is triggered 
Recommended Action 
Check if the E-STOP function of the TP or the system is triggered. 
The E-STOP function should be off. Clear the alarm with Alarm 
Reset. 
 
 

I/O Control (I/O)                                                           DRAStudio User Manual 
6-6 
6 
 
Functional Pause 
The System DI2 is On by default, indicating that the functional pause is not triggered. 
 
When the functional pause is triggered (System DI2 is Off) during jogging, the robot 
pauses while the servo status remains unchanged. In this scenario, you cannot operate 
the robot. 
When the functional pause is triggered during program execution, the program pauses at 
the current execution line and the system displays WA001. 
 
Alarm / Warning 
WA001 (Gating is triggered) 
Probable Cause 
Functional pause is triggered 
Recommended Action 
Untrigger the functional pause and turn on the reset switch 
(System DI3) to clear the alarm. 
You can only reset the functional pause with the System DI3 after the System DI2 is 
switched back to On. Then, the program execution resumes, and the robot continues 
moving. 
Note: 
1. 
The functional pause is only applicable in the Auto and T2 modes, not in the T1 mode. 
2. 
You can also set or read the functional pause status through RL. For details, refer to Section A.2 
Functional pause in RL. 
 
Reset Functional Pause 
Use the System DI3 to reset the functional pause status of the robot. 
 
After you trigger the System DI3 to reset the functional pause status, WA001 is cleared.

toolbar (marked with a red box as follows).

Jog speed: the TCP (Tool Center Point) linear speed is limited under



the project is above 225 mm/sec, the speed is limited at 225 mm/sec. 
For example, when the moving speed is set to 500 mm/sec in the



In the Auto mode, the JOG function is only operable in the DRAStudio 
but not on the TP. 


Important: when the System DI4 and DI5 are both Off, the system displays E1.998, indicating 
that the operation mode is disabled. To clear the alarm, properly wire System DI4 and DI5, and 
then cycle power on the controller. 
Alarm / Warning 
E1.998 (Operation mode has not been activated) 
Probable Cause 
The operation mode is not enabled 
Recommended Action 
Make sure both the contacts of System DI4 and DI5 are connected, 
and then restart the controller.

6.1.2 System DO 
The System DOs allow you to obtain the current status information of the robot. The System 
DO8 is reserved and cannot be used at present. 
 
 
Alarm Status 
Use the System DO1 to output the current alarm status or warning status. The following 
indicates that there are no alarms or warnings. 
 
When an alarm or warning is triggered, the status is shown as follows. After the alarm or 
warning is cleared, you can reset the status with System DI8. 
 
 
 

I/O Control (I/O)                                                           DRAStudio User Manual 
6-10 
6 
 
Servo Status 
Use the System DO2 to output the current servo status. The following indicates that all the 
axes are disabled. 
 
The System DO2 is On after all the axes are enabled. 
 
 
Moving Status 
Use the System DO3 to check whether the robot is moving. 
 
 
 
Functional Pause (Functional pause status) 
Use the System DO4 to check whether the robot is in the functional pause status. 
System DO4 is Off when the functional pause function is triggered (System DI2 is Off). 
 
System DO4 is On when the functional pause function is not triggered (System DI2 is On). 
 
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                           I/O Control (I/O) 
6-11 
6 
 
Project Execution Status 
System DO5 and DO6 are both On when the project is in the RUN status. 
 
System DO5 is On and System DO6 is Off when the project is in the STOP status. 
 
System DO5 is Off and System DO6 is On when the project is in the PAUSE status. 
 
 
Controller Ready Status 
When the System DO7 is On, it indicates that the controller is ready, and the robot is now 
operable.

6.4 MonitorAddress (Memory address mapping) 
The 
 MonitorAddress screen in the 
 I/O page allows the system to automatically map 
multiple sets of Modbus addresses to the specified addresses. The RL instructions only 
supports reading and writing the Modbus addresses of 0x1000 to 0x1FFF and 0x3000 to 0x3FFF. 
To access the information in 0x0000 to 0x0FFF, you need to map them to the readable / 
writable range. 
 
Item 
Name 
Description 
1 
Settings bar 
 
Enable: select the check box to enable the MonitorAddress function. 
 
Decimal: select the check box to have the addresses displayed in 
decimal (the default is hexadecimal). 
 
 
Rate(Int): set the scan time after the MonitorAddress function is 
enabled (range: an integer from 10 to 10000; unit: ms). 
2 
Operation functions 
 
Open: open the MonitorAddress setting file (.txt) with the filename 
“MonitorAddressResult”, which is saved under the default 
installation folder for DRAStudio. 
 
Read: read the settings from the controller and display them in the 
MonitorAddress screen. 
 
Set: save the settings to the setting file on the PC and update them 
to the controller. 
 
Count (Int): set the number of mappings to be used and displayed 
(range: 1 to 64). 
3 
Fields for address 
mapping 
 
Source Buffer*1: source address with the setting range of 0x0000 to 
0x1FFF. 
 
Dest Buffer*1: target address with the setting range of 0x0000 to 
0x033F and 0x1000 to 0x1FFF. 
 
Note*2: you can input descriptions for the usage of the addresses. 
Note: 
*1. 
For the details of the addresses 0x0000 to 0x0FFF, refer to the file "DCS, DCV Modbus Address 
Manual (User).xlsx" located in the [Manual] folder under the DRAStudio installation folder. 
*2. 
When you save the settings, the contents in the Note column are only written to the PC. The 
controller only records the source and target addresses for mapping.

The Z index tab displays the mechanical home position of each robot axis in units of PUU*Note.

7.3 
Hardware Limit 
The Hardware Limit tab displays the motor encoder value (unit: PUU) at the hardware limit of 
each robot axis. When a robot collision occurs, move the robot to the hardware limit and click 
Get Value to check whether the home position is lost. Either Default1 or Default2 should contain 
the encoder values of all applicable axes (except J4 and J6); otherwise, contact Delta.

7.3.1  J4 & J6 axes 
Since the J4 and J6 axes have no hardware limits and their rotation counts cannot be 
calculated, the Hardware Limit function for the two axes is disabled by default. If you use the 
JRC value to obtain the absolute positions and the rotation counts of the J4 and J6 axes, you 
can manually enable the Hardware Limit function for the two axes. 
1. 
Go to the Options tab of the System page, and then select the J4 Enable and J6 Enable 
check boxes in the Hard Limit area. 
 
2. 
Return to the Hardware Limit tab. You can see that the previously grayed out Hardware 
Limit function for the J4 and J6 axes is now enabled.

Homing (Home)                                                           DRAStudio User Manual

7.3.2  Hardware limit posture 
There are multiple hardware limit postures which vary based on the applications; the postures 
must be stable and can be recreated. The following are the default postures of the 4-axis 
SCARA robots and the 6-axis VA robots.

8.2  Payload 
In the Payload tab, you can set the weight to be handled by the end of the robotic arm. The 
robot can achieve optimal performance with a proper setting of the Payload parameters. 
 
Setting steps: 
1. Click 
 (Servo control) in the Quick access toolbar to switch all the motors to the Servo 
Off state. 
2. Properly set the Mass, Distance, and Inertia. 
3. Click Set. 
Important: pay special attention when setting the parameters in the Payload tab, which have 
significant impact on the robot’s moving speed. 
 
8.2.1  Setting the Mass 
Set the weight (unit: kg) to be handled by the end of the robotic arm. If you set the weight too 
high, the robot handles the end effector with higher torque, which leads to inefficiencies. If you 
set the weight too low, the robot may jitter because of insufficient torque. 
The robot’s payload capacity differs from models. For example, in the model number of 
DRV70L7, the last code “7” indicates a payload of 7 kg (15.4 lb). 
 
 
 
DRV70L7 
7 kg

you can enter the X-, Y-, and Z-axis values in the CoGx, CoGy, and CoGz fields respectively.

8.3  Lead & Collision 
In the Lead & Collision tab, you can set the parameters for collision protection to reduce 
damage to the robot when there is a collision. 
8.3.1  Collision Setting 
When detecting a collision, the collision protection brakes the motor and reverses the robot 
moving direction to prevent the robot from continuously moving to the target position, or it may 
cause more serious damage. 
 
Item 
Description 
Collision Duration 
Time 
Set the delay time of triggering the collision protection after a collision occurs.  
Setting range: 2 to 1000; unit: ms. 
Sensitivity 
Set the detection sensitivity in the range of 1 to 100. 
The greater the setting value, the higher the sensitivity; in that case, a minor bump 
or slight contact triggers the collision protection. Conversely, only a major collision 
triggers the collision protection. Adjust the sensitivity as required. 
Enable 
OFF: switch off the collision protection. 
ON: switch on the collision protection. 
When the Mass setting in the Payload tab does not match the actual weight, it 
affects the collision detection. Thus, make sure you have properly set the Mass 
before switching on the collision protection. 
Status 
Shows whether the collision protection is ON. You can also go to the Status 
subpage and click Update to check the status for “Collision Detect”.

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                        Arm

8.4  Gravity 
In the Gravity tab, you can set the Gravity parameters. 
The larger the robot’s workspace, the longer the equivalent moment arm (indicated with double 
arrows as follows), and the greater the moment of force is applied on the robot. In this scenario, 
the motor requires higher torque to handle the end effector weight for avoiding the Z-axis offset 
caused by gravity.

Arm                                                                        DRAStudio User Manual

Item 
Description 
Enabled 
Select this check box to enable gravity compensation. 
Normal (table-mount) /  
Ceiling (ceiling-mount) 
Select the mounting type of the robot as table-mount or ceiling-mount. 
Mass (kg) 
(payload) 
Enter the end effector weight. The robot’s payload capacity differs from 
models. Refer to Section 8.2.1 for details. 
X (mm), Y (mm),  
Z (mm)*Note 
Enter the X-, Y-, and Z-axis distances from the tool’s center of gravity to the 
end of the robotic arm, which can be determined by CAD software tools. 
Refer to Section 8.2.2 for details. 
Important: in the Gravity tab, the distances are in units of mm; in the Payload tab, the 
distances are in units of meters. Pay attention when converting between units.

8.4.2  How to use the Gravity function 
 Set Gravity 
1. Click 
 (Servo control) in the Quick access toolbar to switch all the motors to the 
Servo Off state. 
2. Enter the end effector weight, and the X-, Y-, and Z-axis distances from the tool’s center 
of gravity to the end of the robotic arm. 
3. Click SET. 
 
4. Go to the Status subpage, click Update, and you can find that “Gravity Compensation 
Deformation” is ON. 
 
 Get Gravity 
1. Click GET. 
 
2. Check the Gravity parameters read by the system.

9.2 
Temperature 
Set the temperature protection level and the upper limit of the normal temperature of the motor 
in the Temperature tab. 
 
Name 
Description 
Level 
Set the temperature protection level of the motor. 
 
Default: Warning temp - motor does not decelerate; Alarm temp - motor stops. 
 
Level 1: Warning temp - motor decelerates; Alarm temp - motor pauses until cools down. 
 
Level 2: Warning temp - motor decelerates; Alarm temp - motor stops. 
 
Level 3: Warning temp - motor stops. 
Setting 
Set the upper limit of the normal temperature of the motor. 
The system automatically sets the upper limit of the warning temperature by adding an 
increment of 10°C (50°F) to the upper limit of the normal temperature.

RL User Log 
To use the User Log function, first go to the RL page and input the User Log instructions to the 
Editor area, and then you can use the buttons in the RL User Log tab. Refer to Sections 9.6.1 
and 9.6.2 for details. 
 
Function 
Description

User Log display.

User Log display 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                   Robot Information (Info) 
9-7 
9 
9.6.1 
User Log instructions 
 
print(string or number) 
Specify the data to be retrieved. Each row in the User Log display can record a maximum 
of 128 characters (including symbols and spaces); the exceeded characters are truncated. 
In the RL User Log tab, you can save the retrieved data to the PC. 
Example: 
print("ABC") 
-- Print the string ABC. 
print(123) 
-- Print the number 123. 
print("A,B,C") 
-- Print the string A,B,C. 
 
SetUserLogSize(string or number) 
Set the maximum size (range: 5 KB - 512 KB; default: 512 KB) of the User Log file to be 
saved to the USB drive. 
Example: 
SetUserLogSize("5") 
-- Set the maximum size of the User Log file to 5 KB. 
 
SetUserLogToUSB(string) 
Generate and save a User Log file (.txt or .CSV) to the USB drive connected to the 
controller. 
Example: 
SetUserLogToUSB("UserLog.txt") -- Generate a User Log file “UserLog.txt” and save it to the 
USB drive.

RS232/485, Language, Backup, Recovery, and Options. 
 
10.1 
EtherNet IP 
This tab is for setting the Ethernet IP address, SubNet Mask, and Default Gateway of the robot 
controller. Enter the parameters in the fields and then click Set to complete the setting.

10.2 
RS232/485 
You can choose to use RS-232 or RS-485 for communication. In the RS232/485 tab, set the 
parameters in the Set Communications area, click Set to write the settings, and then restart the 
robot to have the settings take effect. To obtain and show the current settings, click Get in the 
Get Communications area. 
 
Item 
Description 
Transmission Mode 
You can set the Transmission Mode to RS-232 or RS-485. 
Transmission Rate 
You can set the Transmission Rate (baud) as 4800, 9600, 19200, 38400, 
57600, or 115200. 
Protocol 
Set the Protocol based on the needs according to the following table. 
See the following figure for the provided options. 
Communication parameter 
Content 
Data length 
7-bit or 8-bit 
Parity 
N (none), E (even parity), or O (odd parity) 
Stop bit 
1-bit or 2-bit 
Mode 
ASCII or RTU 
 
Modbus Address 
Set the Modbus Address (register address) in the range of 2 to 247. 
Important: if more than one robot is connected, there must be an offset of 2 
for the address of every additional robot, such as 2, 4, 6, 8, and 
so on. 
Modbus Slave/Master 
Set the robot controller as a Slave or a Master.

10.3 
Language 
DRAStudio currently supports 3 languages, including English, 繁體中文 (Traditional Chinese), 
and 简体中文 (Simplified Chinese). Select the language from the drop-down list box, click 
Change, and then restart DRAStudio to have the change take effect. 
 
   
 
 

DRAStudio User Manual                                                                     System 
10-5

10.4 
Backup 
1. Enter a name in the Backup Folder Name field first (only English letters and numbers can 
be used for naming the folder). If necessary, you can add any descriptions in the Notes 
field. The Backup Path is the DROEbackup folder under the installation folder. 
 
2. To back up all the robot data to the PC, click Complete BackupNote. To back up part of the 
data, select the check box(es) for the specific item(s), and then click Option Backup. 
Note: the Recovery function described in Section 10.5 is available only when you back up data with 
the Complete Backup function. 
3. After the backup is complete, the Status column of the table shows “Done”. 
 
Important: make sure the robot connection is stable during backup, or the backup may fail. 
 
 

System                                                                     DRAStudio User Manual 
10-6 
10 
10.5 
Recovery 
The Recovery function is available only when you have backed up data with the Complete 
Backup function in the Backup tab. 
1. Select the backup file from the Backup Name drop-down list box, select the check box(es) 
of the item(s) to be restored in the table, and then click Recovery. 
2. When the specified item(s) in the backup file is successfully restored to the robot controller, 
the Status column of the table shows “Done”. 
 
Important: make sure the robot connection is stable during recovery, or the recovery may fail.

Hard Limit 
Select the J4 Enable and J6 Enable check boxes to enable the Hardware Limit 
function for J4 and J6 axes in the Hardware Limit tab of the Home page.

The 
 License page in the menu of DRAStudio contains three tabs. 
 DRAStudio: verify the license(s) of the function(s) related to the DRAStudio software. 
 Registration: obtain the AuthorityKey (license code) by registering the information of the 
function. 
 DCS/DCV: verify the license(s) of the function(s) related to the controller. 
The advanced functions are optional for you to purchase as needed. After obtaining the 
AuthorityKeys, bind them to DRAStudio or the controller, and then you can operate the 
functions right away. There is no need to re-verify the functions every time DRAStudio is 
updated to a new version.

verify the license once. Contact Delta or the distributor if you want to transfer the verified

A.2  Functional pause in RL 
Two instructions are provided in Delta’s RL for the functional pause operations. 
A.2.1  SafetyMode (Set the status of functional pause) 
This instruction is for switching the functional pause modes. After the functional pause is 
triggered, the robot decelerates to a pause. To resume the robot operation, you need to set the 
system DI3 (functional pause reset) to OFF, ON, and then OFF. 
(1) 
Syntax 
SafetyMode(Mode) 
 Mode 
There are five functional pause modes with the setting value ranging from 1 to 5; the 
default mode is 1. The definitions of each mode are as follows. 
1: when the functional pause is triggered, the robot pauses after completing the current 
motion. The DI/O statuses remain unchanged.

header: A.2.2  SafetyStatus (Read the status of functional pause)
vector_text:
DRAStudio User Manual                                                            Appendix A 
A-7 
A 
A.2.2  SafetyStatus (Read the status of functional pause) 
This instruction is for reading the trigger status of the functional pause and can be used after 
SafetyMode(3) (disable the functional pause). 
(1) 
Syntax 
SafetyStatus(Status) 
 
Status 
When the status of the functional pause is read, the return value can be one of the 
following. 
0: functional pause not triggered. The robot is in normal operation. 
2: functional pause triggered when the program is not running. The motor is in the Servo 
Off state. 
3: functional pause triggered when the program is running. 
(2) 
Example: 
SafetyMode(3) 
 
 
 
 
--Disable the functional pause 
DELAY(5) 
if SafetyStatus()==0 then 
 
 
--Check if the status of functional pause is 0 
print("normal operation") 
 
--If so, print the status in the Output panel 
elseif SafetyStatus()==2 then  
 
--Check if the status of functional pause is 2 
print("functional pause triggered when the program is not running") 
 
 
 
 
 
 
--If so, print the status in the Output panel 
elseif SafetyStatus()==3 then  
 
--Check if the status of functional pause is 3 
print("functional pause triggered when the program is running") 
 
 
 
 
 
 
--If so, print the status in the Output panel 
end

header: recovered and charge the battery pack back to backup
vector_text:
DELTA TEB Series 
 
TEB4810 – 3U 48V/100Ah 
 
Lithium-ion Iron Phosphate (LFP) Battery 
 
 
 
 
This new DELTA 48V battery pack is designed with 
100Ah capacity battery cell of lithium-ion iron phosphate 
chemistry. It provides larger capacity in a compact size 
of 19” rack-mounted 3U chassis. This pack has RS485 
communication and built-in BMS with automatic 
protection and cell balancing to offer safe and most 
efficient operation to customers. 
 
The pack application is developed for Telecom 
equipment power backup. Under normal condition, grid 
AC power supplies to rectifier module and the Telecom 
loads and also charge battery pack. 
 
When the AC power failed, rectifier module stop power 
supply to loads, the battery serves for Telecom 
equipment to ensure the Telecom equipment operation 
normally; when the AC power is switched on again, 
power from rectifier module to Telecom equipment 
recovered and charge the battery pack back to backup 
status. 
 
Key Features 
⚫ RS485 communication output for 
monitoring 
⚫ Built-in automatic protection for 
over-charge, over-discharge and 
over-temperature conditions 
⚫ Built-in BMS with Charging current 
limiting circuit 
⚫ Built-in battery optimal control 
algorithm to manage sleep/wakeup 
and intermittent charging mode to 
improve operation efficiency. 
⚫ Built-in Cell balancing function 
⚫ LED indication of State of Charge 
and Alarm/Run status  
⚫ Compatible with standard Telecom 
rectifiers 
⚫ Maintenance free 
Applications 
⚫ 3G / 4G / 5G 
⚫ Fixed Line 
⚫ Datacom

 章节1: 安全操作指引
安全警示


安装「辅助空调模块」之前，
 请检查电源线插头和插座是否可以正常工作。
 确认所有风扇均已安装牢固，避免运行时脱落。
4 请先断开「辅助空调模块」的电源，以免安装时导致人员受伤
或设备损坏。


安装完成之前，请不要为「辅助空调模块」通电。通电后，手指
和衣物须远离风扇，避免因碰触运行中的风扇运动部件而受伤。


请先将电源线 ( 随机附赠) 连接上「辅助空调模块」后, 再将此电源
线插入电源装置, 且此输入电源的电压范围必需为@6A 。 
另建议加装保护装置，如保险丝或分断开关等进行保护。


为避免触及潜在危险电压导致人员受伤。请勿尝试拆卸此单元。 


请务必按照本手册中的说明，安装和操作「辅助空调模块」。
使用注意事项


此「辅助空调模块」仅可安装于高架地板下方，而高架地板上方
不可放置任何东西，如机柜等，以免阻碍冷却风供应。 


安装「辅助空调模块」的高架地板必须紧临机柜前方。


此「辅助空调模块」仅适用于; 毫米见方且过孔率大于9D 的
高架地板。


若「辅助空调模块」闲置不使用时，請储存在温度)E~;E，
相对湿度D@FD( 不结露状态) 的环境下。


使用前，需将「辅助空调模块」放置在室温至少 小时。
